Methyl-CpG-binding protein 2 - Rett syndrome gene therapy target
Survival motor neuron 1 - spinal muscular atrophy gene therapy target
Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator - cystic fibrosis gene therapy target
Huntingtin - Huntington disease gene therapy/silencing target
Neutrophil cytosolic factor 1 - target of PM359 prime editing therapy for p47-CGD
Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase 1 - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Ornithine transcarbamylase - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Argininosuccinate synthase 1 - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Argininosuccinate lyase - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Arginase 1 - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Solute carrier family 25 member 13 - citrin deficiency gene, prime editing target
N-acetylglutamate synthase - urea cycle disorder gene, prime editing target
Transthyretin - target of Intellia NTLA-2001 in vivo CRISPR therapy for ATTR amyloidosis
Coagulation factor IX - hemophilia B gene therapy target (CSL Behring hemgenix)
B-cell maturation antigen - target for CRISPR allogeneic CAR-T in multiple myeloma
Apolipoprotein E - cardiovascular and Alzheimer disease risk gene, emerging gene therapy target
Coagulation factor VIII - hemophilia A gene therapy target (Roctavian/Valrox approved)
ATP-binding cassette sub-family A member 4 - Stargardt disease gene therapy target
Myosin VIIA - Usher syndrome type 1B gene therapy target
Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome protein - target of first FDA-approved WAS gene therapy
Glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor - Parkinson's disease gene therapy target
Neurturin - neurotrophic factor for Parkinson's disease gene therapy
Thymidine kinase 2 - mitochondrial enzyme, target of first approved TK2 deficiency drug Kygevvi
Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase 1 - target of personalized CRISPR base editing therapy (Baby KJ case)
Low-density lipoprotein receptor - target of Editas EDIT-401 CRISPR therapy showing >90% LDL-C reduction in preclinical studies
Killer cell lectin-like receptor subfamily C member 1 - potential CRISPR target for cancer immunotherapy
Centrosomal protein 290 - Leber congenital amaurosis type 10 gene, antisense oligonucleotide therapy (sepofarsen) in clinical trials
Retinal pigment epithelium-specific protein 65 - Luxturna gene therapy target, 2026 Breakthrough Prize awarded to pioneers
LDLR-APOE pathway - cardiovascular gene therapy targets, in vivo CRISPR editing approaches advancing in 2026
Tumor protein p53 - most frequently mutated gene in cancer, emerging CRISPR target for cancer therapy
Epidermal growth factor receptor - head and neck cancer CRISPR target for restoring chemotherapy sensitivity
Human papillomavirus oncogenes - CRISPR target for destroying virus-infected cells in head and neck cancer
5'-nucleotidase - immunosuppressive enzyme, CRISPR editing combined with gene therapy enhances anti-glioma efficacy
Stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1 - metabolic gene target for CRISPR-based cancer therapy
B-cell antigen CD19 - target for CAR-T gene therapy in B-cell malignancies, multiple FDA-approved products
Interleukin-2 receptor gamma - X-linked SCID gene therapy target, Strimvelis approved in EU
Ryanodine receptor 2 - cardiac gene, potential CRISPR target for arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy
Neurofilament light chain - biomarker for neurodegeneration, target of Encelto gene therapy for pediatric solid tumors
Netrin-1 - neurodevelopmental guidance molecule, therapeutic target in Encelto gene therapy
Arylsulfatase A - enzyme deficient in metachromatic leukodystrophy, target of LENMELDY gene therapy

key_findings: To evaluate the efficacy and safety of ADVM-022, an AAV.7m8-delivered aflibercept gene therapy, in subjects with diabetic macular edema.
Nurses play an essential role in the gene therapy pathway for haemophilia, including education, care coordination, bleed management, and long-term follow-up.
Their responsibilities span from eligibility assessment and patient education to peri-infusion care, adverse event detection, and psychosocial support.
Gene therapy for haemophilia requires well-trained nurses to ensure safe delivery, effective follow-up, and high-quality patient support.
Nurses are crucial for generating real-world evidence through registries and research to evaluate the long-term safety and effectiveness of gene therapy.
In vivo CAR gene delivery has demonstrated feasibility and therapeutic potential in recent clinical reports, offering an alternative to the complex ex vivo manufacturing of CAR-T cells.
Nurses are critical for generating real-world evidence through registries and research to evaluate the long-term safety and effectiveness of gene therapy.
AAV9-Ab immunoglobulin G titers vary by age, with a higher likelihood of titers ≥1:100 in the 0-month age group compared to others.
Titers decline up to 12 months of age and then steadily increase from 12 to 24 months.
A greater proportion of individuals from Russia had AAV9-Ab titers ≥1:100 compared to other countries.
RAAVioli is a robust and versatile computational method for the comprehensive analysis of AAV integration sites and vector rearrangements across long- and short-read sequencing platforms.
Short-read sequencing provided superior throughput, uncovering more integration sites and enabling precise quantification of clonal abundance, which is essential for evaluating gene therapy safety.
Benchmarking analyses highlighted RAAVioli's higher sensitivity and accuracy relative to existing tools, establishing it as a reliable and scalable resource for AAV-based gene therapy studies.
Computational approaches, including machine learning, are enabling the design of finely engineered viral vectors for gene therapy by exploring vast protein sequence spaces beyond traditional methods.
These computational methods support viral vector bioengineering by targeting stability, structural modeling, functional prediction, and generating novel variants.
The field is moving toward more integrated and holistic design strategies, with a transformative potential for the next generation of viral gene therapies.
Lyophilization of AAV vectors with excipients like methyl cellulose (MC) and dileucine (diLeu) maintains their gene expression activity after reconstitution.
AAV vectors lyophilized with MC showed similar gene expression patterns in mice as non-lyophilized vectors, with no body weight loss.
The combination of diLeu with MC prolonged the gene expression activity of lyophilized AAV vectors for at least 4 weeks at room temperature.
Genome editing has evolved from early complex methods like ZFNs and TALENs to the dominant, simpler, and more efficient CRISPR-Cas9 system.

Advanced variants like base editors and prime editors enable precise single-nucleotide changes and insertions without double-strand breaks, while emerging tools expand manipulation to RNA and epigenetic modifications.

Despite challenges like off-target effects and immunogenicity, CRISPR-derived technologies are at the forefront of next-generation genetic medicine, driving progress toward precise and programmable therapies.

SLC6A1-related disorder is defined by a specific functional loss of GABA transport, enabling a focused translational pipeline. Adeno-associated virus serotype 9-mediated gene replacement normalized EEG abnormalities in preclinical models. 4-phenylbutyrate, a pharmacological chaperone, has demonstrated seizure reduction in 80% of treated patients.

SLC6A1-related disorder is defined by a specific functional loss of GABA transport, enabling a focused translational pipeline. 4-phenylbutyrate, a pharmacological chaperone, has demonstrated seizure reduction in 80% and seizure freedom in 40% of treated patients. Adeno-associated virus serotype 9-mediated gene replacement normalized EEG abnormalities in preclinical models and has entered a Phase I/II human trial.

SLC6A1-related disorder is defined by a specific functional loss of GABA transport, enabling a focused translational pipeline. 4-phenylbutyrate, a pharmacological chaperone, has demonstrated seizure reduction in 80% of patients. Adeno-associated virus-mediated gene replacement normalized EEG abnormalities in preclinical models and has entered a Phase I/II human trial.

SLC6A1-related disorder is defined by a specific functional loss of GABA transport, enabling a focused translational pipeline. Adeno-associated virus serotype 9-mediated gene replacement normalized EEG abnormalities in preclinical models. 4-phenylbutyrate, a pharmacological chaperone, has demonstrated seizure reduction in 80% and seizure freedom in 40% of treated patients.
Pathogenic heterozygous loss-of-function variants of SLC6A1 cause a developmental and epileptic encephalopathy characterized by early-onset epilepsy, intellectual disability, and autistic features.
4-phenylbutyrate restores GAT-1 trafficking in endoplasmic reticulum-retained variants and has demonstrated seizure reduction in 80% and seizure freedom in 40% of treated patients.

Adeno-associated virus serotype 9-mediated gene replacement normalized electroencephalography abnormalities in preclinical models, with the first Phase I/II human trial (NCT07173153) dosing its initial patient in December 2025.
Insect-Cell-Produced AAV2 Vectors Activate Myeloid Innate Immune Response Featuring NETosis.
The FDA has approved the OTOF gene therapy Otarmeni, marking a significant advancement in hearing care.
This approval highlights the potential of gene therapy to treat genetic disorders, with emerging therapies like gene therapy targeting LAMP2 being considered for Danon disease.
RNase P ribozymes, derived from E. coli, are a novel class of RNA-based gene targeting agents that can degrade target mRNAs to suppress gene expression.
Engineered RNase P ribozymes have been developed to more effectively suppress gene expression of HSV-1 and KSHV.
These ribozymes show potential as therapeutics to treat infections and associated diseases caused by HSV-1 and KSHV in the oral cavity.

Noncoding RNAs (ncRNAs) are promising biomarkers and therapeutic targets for cardiovascular diseases due to their critical roles in molecular mechanisms and consistent dysregulation in these conditions.
RNA-based technologies have shown potent efficacy in preclinical studies for targeting cardiovascular disease pathways.
Ongoing clinical trials are evaluating RNA-based drugs, but challenges in delivery, specificity, toxicity, and cardiac uptake must be overcome for widespread clinical application.
RNase P ribozymes, derived from E. coli, are engineered to degrade target mRNAs and suppress gene expression of HSV-1 and KSHV.
This approach shows promise as a novel therapeutic strategy to inhibit viral infection and treat associated oral diseases.
The review summarizes recent advances in using these ribozymes as a potential alternative to current FDA-approved therapies.
Noncoding RNAs (ncRNAs) are promising biomarkers and therapeutic targets for cardiovascular diseases due to their critical roles in regulating molecular mechanisms.
RNA-based technologies have demonstrated potent efficacy in preclinical studies and are being evaluated in ongoing clinical trials.
Challenges related to delivery, specificity, toxicity, and cardiac uptake must be addressed for widespread clinical application.
RNase P ribozymes, derived from E. coli, are a novel class of RNA-based gene targeting agents that can degrade target mRNAs to suppress gene expression and inhibit viral infection.
Engineered RNase P ribozymes have been developed to more effectively suppress the expression of HSV-1 and KSHV genes.
These ribozymes represent a potential therapeutic strategy to treat infections and associated diseases caused by HSV-1 and KSHV in the oral cavity.
RNA-based technologies have demonstrated potent efficacy in preclinical studies, and ongoing clinical trials are evaluating RNA-based drugs for cardiovascular treatment.
Significant challenges related to delivery, specificity, toxicity, and cardiac uptake must be addressed to enable widespread clinical application of RNA-based therapies.
These ribozymes represent a novel class of RNA-based gene targeting molecules that can inhibit viral infection.
The review summarizes their potential as a therapeutic strategy for oral herpesvirus infections, addressing limitations of current FDA-approved therapies.
RNA-based technologies have demonstrated potent efficacy in preclinical studies for targeting cardiovascular disease pathways.
RNase P ribozymes, derived from E. coli, are a novel class of RNA-based gene targeting agents that can degrade target mRNAs to suppress gene expression and inhibit infection of HSV-1 and KSHV.
Engineered RNase P ribozymes have been developed to more effectively suppress gene expression and show potential as therapeutics for oral infections caused by these viruses.
Mutant IDH1 glioma cells exhibit reduced CD73 expression due to DNA hypermethylation, leading to lower adenosine levels.
CD73 inhibition alone did not improve survival in wtIDH1 glioma-bearing mice.
Combining CD73 blockade with immune-stimulatory Ad-TK/Ad-Flt3L gene therapy significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy and increased anti-glioma effector T cell activity.
Furin-mediated cleavage inactivates LPL in vivo.
Engineered furin-resistant LPL lowers plasma triglyceride levels more effectively than wild-type LPL.
Furin-resistant LPL does not cause adverse effects like hepatic steatosis.
Engineered furin-resistant LPL lowers longitudinal plasma triglyceride levels.
Furin-resistant LPL does not cause adverse effects such as hepatic steatosis.
Loss of CLN3 causes defective synaptic vesicle release and reduced synaptic strength, reflecting impairments in both pre- and postsynaptic function.
AAV9-mediated gene rescue at early disease stages corrected preexisting synaptic defects and restored function.
Early synaptic deficits provide sensitive and robust functional readouts for preclinical research.
A 100 kDa serum titin fragment was identified as a biomarker for dystrophinopathies.
The concentration of this fragment negatively correlated with dystrophin expression in mdx mice treated with AAV9-CK8-μDys5 gene therapy.
This fragment shows a steeper age-related decline in DMD patients compared to BMD patients, suggesting its potential as a pharmacodynamic biomarker.
The oligosaccharide H3N2b was identified as a highly specific and sensitive biomarker for diagnosing GM1 gangliosidosis across plasma, urine, and CSF.
H3N2b levels correlated with disease severity and decreased following AAV9-mediated GLB1 gene therapy, closely paralleling changes in β-galactosidase activity.
This demonstrates H3N2b's utility as a pharmacodynamic biomarker for monitoring therapeutic response.
Promoter strength and cassette architecture are dominant determinants of AAV-AAT potency.
Full-length, single-stranded vectors with robust regulatory elements provide the highest expression.
Codon optimization and modifications to polyadenylation signals or enhancer combinations did not improve expression.
Epigenetic mechanisms are a core regulatory factor in osteoporosis, linking genetic predisposition and environmental influences.
Future research will focus on miRNAs in OP epigenetics and identifying therapeutic targets.
Emerging therapeutic strategies include gene therapy, stem cell-based interventions, and small-molecule targeted compounds.

AAV-mediated human L-opsin gene therapy restored cone function, replenished phototransduction proteins, and partially regenerated cone outer segment membrane structure in an all-cone mouse model of BCM.
Treatment was effective in both younger (1 month) and older (5 months) mice, with rescue effects persisting for at least 5 months post-treatment.
These findings suggest that opsin-deficient cones remain viable targets for gene therapy, supporting its clinical potential for BCM patients.

Engineered Cross-Correction, using a secretable frataxin delivered via a single intra-CSF AAV injection, achieved broad protein repletion in the heart, dorsal root ganglia, and cerebellum in mouse and non-human primate models.
This approach rescued both cardiac and neurological phenotypes in FA mouse models, marking the first demonstration of dual correction with a single, minimally invasive administration.
The benefits were achieved without widespread transduction, reducing vector burden and associated toxicity.
Liver-specific miR122 effectively suppressed AAV9-driven liver expression while preserving adipose tissue targeting efficiency.
AAVMYO, in combination with a muscle-specific promoter and target sites for miR208a, confined transgene expression to skeletal muscle while successfully de-targeting the liver and heart.
Both miRNA-based approaches outperformed conditionally self-silencing constructs that utilized artificial miRNAs.
Intracisternal IGF-1 gene delivery attenuated early anxiety-like behavior in a 6-OHDA rat model of parkinsonism.
However, IGF-1 overexpression did not prevent the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the ventral tegmental area.
This demonstrates a dissociation between the behavioral effects of IGF-1 and its impact on dopaminergic neurodegeneration.

Current treatments for Gaucher disease include enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) and substrate reduction therapy (SRT). New and emerging therapies include small molecule drugs, gene therapy, and nanotechnology. Future efforts should focus on brain-penetrant therapies for neuronopathic GD and GBA1-related Parkinson disease.
The GoCas12m-FokI chimeric nuclease is a compact gene editor that achieves high-efficiency editing at clinically relevant targets with no detectable off-target activity.
Its small size facilitates delivery via adeno-associated virus and other cargo-limited vectors.
The system offers a powerful alternative for therapeutic genome editing and a promising tool for in vivo gene therapy applications.
Centripetal retinal degeneration in rod-cone dystrophies follows an exponential decay.
A significant reversal of the hyperautofluorescent ring was observed following RPGR gene therapy, correlating with improvements in retinal sensitivity.
Neurogene's NGN-401 AAV gene therapy for Rett syndrome showed significant clinical improvement in Phase I/II trial with favorable safety profile.
Taysha Gene Therapies TSHA-102 demonstrated durable clinical improvements in Rett syndrome patients at 48 weeks post-treatment.
Systemic gene therapy approaches for Rett syndrome are advancing with novel dosing strategies to manage MECP2 overexpression toxicity.
FDA approved Otarmeni (OTOF gene therapy) in 2025, marking the first gene therapy for genetic hearing loss.
Clinical trials showed restoration of hearing in children with OTOF-related auditory neuropathy.
This approval represents a major milestone for inner ear gene therapy delivery.
Multiple gene therapy approaches for cystic fibrosis are in clinical development using AAV and mRNA-LNP delivery.
Inhaled mRNA therapy showed CFTR protein expression in airway cells of CF patients.
CRISPR-based gene correction strategies for CF are advancing in preclinical models.
Roche/Genentech tominersen antisense therapy showed dose-dependent HTT lowering but Phase III trial was halted due to unfavorable risk-benefit.
UniQure AMT-130 AAV5 gene therapy showed sustained NfL reduction and potential clinical benefit at 24 months in Phase I/II trial.
Wave Life Sciences SELECT-HD allele-selective ASO showed target engagement in Phase Ib/II trial.
Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) remains the standard gene therapy for SMA, with long-term data showing sustained SMN protein expression.
Newborn screening programs have enabled earlier treatment with significantly improved outcomes.
Next-generation AAV capsids are being developed to address pre-existing immunity and improve CNS delivery.
FDA approved Kresladi (marnetegragene autotemcel) on March 26, 2026 — first gene therapy for severe LAD-I
Uses patient's own hematopoietic stem cells modified to introduce functional ITGB2 gene copies
Restores CD18 and CD11a cell surface expression in white blood cells including neutrophils
Granted Orphan Drug, Rare Pediatric Disease, Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy, and Fast Track designations
First personalized in vivo CRISPR therapy for an infant with CPS1 deficiency — developed in just 6 months
LNP-delivered CRISPR base editing therapy administered via IV infusion with no adverse effects across 3 doses
Named Nature's 2025 Breakthrough of the Year for systemic personalized gene-editing therapy
Acuitas LNP formulation (ALC-0307) validated for next-generation gene-editing delivery
NovaIscB: compact RNA-guided enzyme re-engineered from bacteria, 1/3 the size of Cas9
Over 100x more active in human cells than original IscB, with good specificity
Small enough to fit in a single AAV vector — solving delivery challenge of bulkier Cas9 tools
OMEGAoff tool created from NovaIscB demonstrated lasting cholesterol reduction in mice via AAV delivery
NTLA-2001 global Phase III trial initiated January 2025, dosing 40 participants with higher-dose treatment
Intellia hopes to have the treatment commercially available in 2027 pending positive Phase III results
First participant dosed in Phase III in January 2025
Prior Phase I data showed >90% serum TTR reduction with single dose
As of spring 2025, CASGEVY approved in US, UK, EU, Switzerland, Canada, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, UAE
16 of 17 SCD patients free of vaso-occlusive crises; 25 of 27 TDT patients no longer transfusion dependent
50 active treatment sites opened across North America, EU, and Middle East
Medicaid and NHS reimbursement arrangements progressing
FDA approved Otarmeni (OTOF gene therapy) in 2025 for genetic hearing loss
First gene therapy approved for hearing loss, marking major milestone
Demonstrates gene therapy potential beyond retinal and hematologic diseases

Includes CAR-T products like Kymriah, Yescarta, Tecartus, Breyanzi, Abecma, and Carvykti

FDA approved Kygevvi (doxecitine and doxribtimine) in 2025 as the first drug for thymidine kinase 2 (TK2) deficiency, a very rare mitochondrial disease
TK2 deficiency causes progressive muscle weakness and respiratory failure; Kygevvi addresses the underlying nucleoside depletion
First-ever approved treatment for this ultra-rare mitochondrial disorder
FDA approved Encelto (revakinagene taroretcel-lwey) in 2025, the first-ever gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome
Uses encapsulated cell technology for sustained protein delivery

CTX310 represents a potential one-time treatment for cardiovascular risk reduction

Enables systematic evaluation of gene function in immune cells for multiple diseases simultaneously
Tool could accelerate discovery of new therapeutic targets for autoimmune and inflammatory conditions

Encelto uses AAV2 vector to deliver therapeutic genes directly to tumor sites

Proof-of-concept studies supporting broad UCD treatment
FDA plausible mechanism framework enables new regulatory pathway
A CRISPR/Cas9-based gene therapy was developed to simultaneously knockdown Keap1 and PHD2, activating the Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways to combat oxidative stress in diabetic wounds.
Delivery was achieved using multifunctional peptide-modified lysine dendrimers (MsRNPs) that showed excellent biocompatibility and efficient gene editing in vitro.
A single topical application of the polyplexes in a diabetic mouse model robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis.
Delivery was achieved using multifunctional peptide-modified lysine dendrimers (MsRNPs), which showed excellent biocompatibility and efficient gene editing in vitro.
A single CRISPR/Cas9 system was engineered to simultaneously knockdown Keap1 and PHD2, leading to synergistic activation of Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways.
This therapy, delivered by peptide dendrimers, reduced ROS levels and robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis in a diabetic mouse model.
A single CRISPR/Cas9 system was engineered for the simultaneous knockdown of Keap1 and PHD2, leading to synergistic activation of Nrf2 and HIF-1α pathways.
Topical application of the MsRNP-delivered polyplexes in a diabetic mouse model robustly accelerated wound closure, enhanced collagen deposition, and promoted angiogenesis.
This study establishes a novel combinatorial gene-editing strategy and a versatile nanoplatform for treating oxidative stress-related pathologies.
The EGOLD method was developed for high-throughput detection of CRISPR off-target effects and identification of chromatin context bias.
Off-target effects of CRISPR/Cas9 and base editors strongly correlate with open chromatin regions.
Incorporating chromatin context data into machine learning models significantly improves off-target prediction accuracy.
Genome editing has evolved from early complex methods like ZFNs and TALENs to the dominant, simpler, and more efficient CRISPR-Cas9 system.

Advanced variants like base editors and prime editors enable precise single-nucleotide changes and insertions without double-strand breaks, while emerging tools expand manipulation to RNA and epigenetic modifications.

Despite challenges like off-target effects and immunogenicity, CRISPR-derived technologies are at the forefront of next-generation genetic medicine, driving progress toward precise and programmable therapies.

Lentiviral vectors are being engineered for precise targeting in cancer therapy to overcome limitations of broad tropism and serum sensitivity. Advances include pseudotyping with natural viral envelopes and next-generation retargeting strategies to enhance specificity for immune and tumor cells. Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of gene editing complexes like CRISPR-Cas9, base-editing, and prime-editing.
Lentiviral vectors can be engineered with various pseudotypes and targeting strategies to achieve precise delivery to specific cell types like immune and tumor cells.

Next-generation retargeting innovations, including engineered receptor-binding domains and VSV-G mutants, enhance specificity for T cells, B cells, hematopoietic stem cells, and tumor-associated antigens.
Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of CRISPR-Cas9, base-editing, and prime-editing complexes for precision oncology.
Lentiviral vectors (LVs) are being engineered for precise targeting in cancer therapy to overcome limitations of broad tropism and serum sensitivity.
Advanced pseudotyping strategies using natural and engineered envelopes, along with retargeting innovations, enhance specificity for immune and tumor cells.
Engineered virus-like particles (VLPs) are emerging as precision tools for in vivo delivery of CRISPR-based editing complexes for targeted tumor modification and cell-restricted gene editing.
Organoids provide physiologically relevant 3D models for biomedical research, addressing limitations of 2D cultures and reducing species-specific discrepancies.
Organoids are valuable for propagating hard-to-culture pathogens like human rhinovirus C and norovirus, and for studying viral pathogenesis of SARS-CoV-2 and Zika virus.
Organoids have translational utility for antiviral drug screening and preclinical assessment, and can be integrated with technologies like CRISPR gene editing and 3D bioprinting.
AI-driven computational models significantly enhance prediction accuracy, algorithmic efficiency, and translational potential in CRISPR-Cas systems for genetic diseases.
Deep learning, machine learning, and reinforcement learning approaches are key to these improvements.
Ethical challenges, algorithmic bias, and data security remain critical barriers to clinical adoption.

Adenoviral vectors are a highly researched gene therapy platform with high transduction efficiency, large transgene capacity, and broad tropism, but their clinical use is limited by immunogenicity, hepatotoxicity, scalability issues, and lack of durable expression.
Recent vector engineering innovations, such as rare serotypes, capsid modifications, and third-generation vectors, aim to overcome these challenges.
AdVs are being integrated with other technologies like CRISPR-Cas9, nanotechnology, and AI to enhance their precision and adaptability in treating genetic disorders and cancer.
A single nuclei RNA-seq study was performed on the lymphoid organ of Litopenaeus vannamei to understand the impact of WSSV infection.
The study constructed the first penaeid shrimp lymphoid organ cell atlas and identified marker genes for different cell types.
Multiple genes of interest were identified as potential targets for CRISPR gene editing to develop WSSV-resistant shrimp stocks.
Autologous CAR-T cells derived from glioblastoma patients show functional deficits and reduced efficacy compared to healthy donor cells in a pre-clinical model.
Allogeneic CAR-T cells, generated using CRISPR to knock out the TCR, demonstrate comparable pre-clinical efficacy to autologous models.
This work suggests that allogeneic approaches may be a more effective alternative to autologous CAR-T therapy for glioblastoma.
The GoCas12m-FokI chimeric nuclease is a compact gene editor that achieves high-efficiency editing at clinically relevant targets with no detectable off-target activity.
Its small size facilitates delivery via adeno-associated virus and other cargo-limited vectors.
The system offers a powerful alternative for therapeutic genome editing and a promising tool for in vivo gene therapy applications.
CRISPR Therapeutics CTX310 demonstrated deep and durable reductions of triglycerides (TG) and ANGPTL3 protein in 2025 clinical data.
In vivo CRISPR base editing of ANGPTL3 and PCSK9 showed sustained lipid lowering in primate studies.
Verve Therapeutics VERVE-101 base editing of PCSK9 entered Phase Ib HEART-1 trial for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.
Verve VERVE-101 uses base editing to inactivate PCSK9 in the liver, showing >80% PCSK9 reduction in Phase Ib trial.
Beam Therapeutics BEAM-101 for sickle cell disease uses base editing to reactivate fetal hemoglobin.
Base editing avoids double-strand breaks, reducing risk of chromosomal rearrangements compared to standard CRISPR-Cas9.
CASGEVY (exagamglogene autotemcel) approved for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia, the first CRISPR-based therapy.
Long-term follow-up shows sustained fetal hemoglobin induction and transfusion independence in beta-thalassemia patients.
CASGEVY gaining commercial momentum in 2026 with expanded manufacturing capacity and new treatment centers.
Intellia's NTLA-2001 in vivo CRISPR therapy showed >90% serum TTR reduction with single dose in Phase I trial.
This represents the first systemic in vivo CRISPR gene editing therapy in humans, delivered via lipid nanoparticles.
Nature named this systemic personalized gene-editing therapy as a 2025 Breakthrough of the Year.

GalNAc-modified LNP delivery targeting hepatocytes
Phase 1 results: 74.4% PCSK9 reduction and 52.3% LDL-C reduction at 24 weeks (0.6 mg/kg cohort)
No grade ≥3 adverse events

PM359: first-ever clinical use of prime editing in humans
Corrects delGT in NCF1 gene using prime editing (no double-strand breaks)
Two patients treated: 68% and 91% corrected alleles in colony-forming cells
Near-normal neutrophil NADPH oxidase activity restored within 1 month
Stable for at least 6 months, no serious adverse events
